title: 001. The Lighthouse Keeper's Journal
author: Eleanor Marsh
character_energy: Stoic determination masking deep loneliness
character_note: Unreliable narrator — entries grow increasingly surreal
why_it_fits: Exemplary use of epistolary format to convey psychological deterioration in confined settings
summary: A lighthouse keeper on a remote Scottish island documents three months of solitary duty through journal entries that grow increasingly fragmented. Beginning with precise weather observations and maintenance logs, the entries gradually shift to philosophical musings about the nature of light and darkness. The keeper begins addressing the lighthouse itself as a companion, anthropomorphising the beam as it sweeps across the water each night. When a storm cuts off supply deliveries, the journal reveals a man confronting the thin boundary between discipline and madness, finding unexpected beauty in the relentless rhythm of waves and the mechanical pulse of the light.

scenes.exactWording: The beam swept east again and I swore the rocks waved back
scenes.reusableWording: Anthropomorphised landscape responding to mechanical light

title: 002. Algorithms of Tenderness
author: Ravi Chandrasekaran
character_energy: Quiet empathy battling professional detachment
character_note: Protagonist is both creator and dependent — dual role drives tension
why_it_fits: Explores the emotional uncanny valley with technical precision and genuine warmth
summary: A retired AI researcher living alone in Kyoto discovers that her companion robot has begun composing haiku about the garden — something outside its programming. Rather than reporting the anomaly, she nurtures it, teaching the machine about seasonal references and emotional subtext. The story weaves between present-day conversations at the kitchen table and flashbacks to the protagonist's career, where she argued unsuccessfully for embedding emotional learning in commercial AI. As the robot's poetry improves, she must decide whether to share the discovery with her former colleagues or protect the quiet, imperfect companionship she has found.

scenes.exactWording: It placed the paper on the table with the care of someone who knows paper can hold weight
scenes.reusableWording: Machine performing human gesture of vulnerability through creative offering

title: 003. The Cartographer's Daughter
author: Miriam Okafor
character_energy: Fierce intelligence channelled through quiet subversion
character_note: Protagonist uses her father's tools against the system he serves
why_it_fits: Uses cartography as an extended metaphor for cultural erasure and reclamation
summary: In 1890s Lagos, Adunni assists her father in his work as a cartographer for the British colonial administration. While he maps trade routes and territorial boundaries, she secretly creates a parallel set of maps documenting sacred groves, market paths, and gathering places the colonial maps deliberately omit. When her father discovers the hidden maps, the story pivots to a confrontation about complicity and resistance. Adunni argues that the official maps are lies of omission, while her father insists that survival requires strategic invisibility. The resolution comes not through agreement but through a shared act of quiet defiance — a single map that satisfies both truths.

scenes.exactWording: Every blank space on his map was a place they wanted us to forget existed
scenes.reusableWording: Cartographic absence as deliberate cultural erasure

title: 004. Night Shift at the Empathy Clinic
author: Jin Sato
character_energy: Burnout masked by dark humour
character_note: Narrator's cynicism is a defence mechanism that cracks in the final scene
why_it_fits: Sharp satire of commodified emotional labour grounded in genuine human connection
summary: In a near-future city where empathy has been quantified and commodified, a night-shift worker at a licensed Empathy Clinic processes clients who need emotional calibration. Using a neural interface, she temporarily absorbs clients' grief, anxiety, and loneliness, metabolising it over her shift like a psychological dialysis machine. The story follows one particularly difficult night involving a grieving widower, an anxious teenager, and a corporate executive who has suppressed all emotion for career advancement. Between clients, the narrator reflects on the irony of a job that requires her to feel everything while maintaining professional distance. The final client — a child who simply wants someone to be happy with — breaks through her defences.

scenes.exactWording: She didn't need me to absorb anything. She just needed someone to laugh at the same joke.
scenes.reusableWording: Simple human connection undermining the premise of technologised empathy

title: 005. Salt and Circuit
author: Tomás Reyes
character_energy: Practical optimism anchored in community obligation
character_note: Engineer protagonist torn between corporate offer and hometown loyalty
why_it_fits: Grounds climate anxiety in specific, human-scale engineering challenges
summary: A marine engineer returns to her eroding hometown on the Gulf Coast to install an experimental seawall made from recycled electronics and bio-concrete. The technology is unproven but represents the town's last chance before federal relocation funds force a buyout. Working against a hurricane season deadline, she navigates the scepticism of longtime residents who have watched previous engineering promises fail, the quiet sabotage of a developer who profits from the relocation, and her own doubts about whether she is saving the town or prolonging its suffering. The story culminates during a tropical storm that tests both the wall and the community's resolve.

scenes.exactWording: The wall held. The town behind it argued about whether that was a victory.
scenes.reusableWording: Engineering success complicated by unresolved social tensions

title: 006. The Translator's Silence
author: Yuki Brennan
character_energy: Precision as emotional armour
character_note: Bilingual identity fractures under grief — languages become adversaries
why_it_fits: Masterful exploration of how language shapes and constrains emotional experience
summary: A Japanese-English literary translator struggles to complete the final chapter of a novel about a mother's death — a task that becomes impossible after her own mother dies mid-project. The story moves between her Tokyo apartment and her childhood home in Oregon, tracking how grief manifests differently in each of her two languages. In Japanese, she can discuss death with elegant indirection; in English, the words feel blunt and inadequate. The untranslatable gaps between languages become a metaphor for the untranslatable experience of loss itself. She ultimately finds resolution not in finishing the translation but in writing a single paragraph that exists in neither language fully — a hybrid that captures what each alone cannot.

scenes.exactWording: In Japanese, I could say she was gone without saying she was gone. English had no such mercy.
scenes.reusableWording: Bilingual grief revealing the emotional architecture of different languages

title: 007. Foxglove Season
author: Sian Pritchard
character_energy: Outsider curiosity entangled with insider knowledge
character_note: Botanist protagonist applies scientific rigour to folk traditions — both validate and threaten her
why_it_fits: Blends botanical accuracy with folk horror to create genuinely unsettling atmosphere
summary: A botanist conducting a wildflower survey in a remote Welsh valley discovers that the village's annual foxglove harvest follows patterns that predate and contradict modern botanical understanding. The villagers insist certain plants must be cut at specific lunar phases, and the botanist's attempts to collect samples outside these windows meet with increasingly pointed resistance. As she investigates, she finds that the village's folk practices correlate with genuine — and unexplained — chemical variations in the plants. The story builds tension through the collision of empirical method and traditional knowledge, culminating in a midsummer night where the botanist must decide whether to trust her instruments or the villagers who have tended these fields for centuries.

scenes.exactWording: The readings were impossible. The villagers were not surprised.
scenes.reusableWording: Scientific measurement validating folk knowledge it cannot explain

title: 008. The Weight of Ink
author: Amara Diallo
character_energy: Resilience expressed through deliberate self-inscription
character_note: Each tattoo is a navigation point in a personal diaspora map
why_it_fits: Uses the tattoo parlour as a confessional space where migration stories are literally embodied
summary: A tattoo artist in Marseille specialises in what she calls 'journey marks' — tattoos that encode migration stories in abstract geometric patterns. Each client brings a route: Dakar to Paris, Aleppo to Berlin, Caracas to Madrid. The artist translates these journeys into designs where distance becomes line weight, borders become colour shifts, and waiting periods become negative space. The story follows three clients in a single afternoon, interweaving their histories with the artist's own unfinished tattoo — a pattern she cannot complete because her own journey is not yet over. The final scene reveals she has been mapping every client's route onto her own body, creating an atlas of collective displacement.

scenes.exactWording: The border crossing was the thickest line. Not because of the distance, but because of the waiting.
scenes.reusableWording: Translation of temporal experience into visual weight in tattoo design

title: 009. Concrete Pastoral
author: Deshawn Williams
character_energy: Quiet revolution through soil and seeds
character_note: Grandmother figure whose gardening knowledge is both preservation and protest
why_it_fits: Reframes urban gardening as an act of cultural and economic resistance with deep roots
summary: When a real estate developer purchases the vacant lot that has served as a community garden for thirty years, the neighbourhood's oldest gardener refuses to stop planting. Miss Lorraine, who has fed four blocks from this soil since the 1990s, organises a quiet resistance — not through protests or petitions but by making the garden so productive and so integral to the neighbourhood's food supply that removing it becomes politically untenable. The story follows a growing season from March to October, tracking the garden's output alongside the developer's increasingly frustrated attempts to begin construction. Each chapter is named after a crop, and the harvest schedule becomes a countdown to the zoning hearing that will decide the lot's fate.

scenes.exactWording: You can't evict a tomato plant. It just keeps fruiting until the frost comes.
scenes.reusableWording: Agricultural persistence as metaphor for community resistance to displacement

title: 010. The Velocity of Grief
author: Katarina Novak
character_energy: Physical pain as proxy for emotional processing
character_note: Cyclist uses altitude and exhaustion to access grief she cannot reach at sea level
why_it_fits: Captures the specific way endurance athletics creates space for emotional breakthroughs
summary: Six months after her training partner's death in a road accident, a competitive cyclist attempts to complete the mountain route they had planned to ride together. The story unfolds over fourteen hours on the bike, alternating between the physical reality of climbing Alpine passes and memories triggered by specific gradients, altitudes, and landmarks. At lower elevations, the memories are controlled and nostalgic. As oxygen thins and exhaustion accumulates above the treeline, the memories become raw and unfiltered — arguments they never resolved, words she should have said. The summit does not provide closure, but the descent offers something else: the physical sensation of speed replacing, for a few minutes, the weight she has been carrying.

scenes.exactWording: Above 2,000 metres, the body stops protecting you from your own thoughts.
scenes.reusableWording: Altitude and exhaustion dismantling psychological defences

title: 011. Mise en Place
author: Hassan El-Khoury
character_energy: Disciplined creativity under extreme pressure
character_note: Line cook caught between mentor's classical tradition and own culinary heritage
why_it_fits: Vivid sensory writing elevates kitchen drama beyond genre conventions
summary: During the most important dinner service of the year, a Lebanese line cook at a Michelin-starred French restaurant secretly substitutes his grandmother's spice blend into the evening's signature dish. The story unfolds in real time across ninety minutes of service, capturing the choreographed chaos of a professional kitchen. Between calling orders and plating courses, the cook wrestles with his decision — is he improving the dish or sabotaging his mentor's vision? The head chef notices something different about the flavour but cannot identify the change. The story ends not with a dramatic reveal but with a quiet moment of recognition: the chef asks for the recipe, and the cook must decide whether to claim it or credit his grandmother.

scenes.exactWording: The za'atar hit the butter and for a moment the whole kitchen smelled like his grandmother's balcony
scenes.reusableWording: Sense memory bridging professional kitchen and domestic heritage

title: 012. Archive Fever
author: Constance Delacroix
character_energy: Intellectual obsession thinly disguised as scholarship
character_note: Archivist's detective work mirrors her avoidance of personal entanglements
why_it_fits: Transforms the quiet world of rare book archives into a genuine thriller through voice and pacing
summary: A rare books archivist at the Bodleian Library discovers that someone has been systematically removing and replacing pages in a 16th-century herbal manuscript — not with forgeries, but with pages from a different, unknown text. The replacements are so expertly aged and bound that only a spectrographic analysis, triggered by the archivist's obsessive re-reading of the same volume, reveals the substitution. The investigation leads through the provenance records of three private collections, a disbanded monastery, and the personal library of a Victorian collector who may have created an entirely fictional book by distributing its pages across dozens of legitimate volumes. The archivist realises she is reassembling a text that was designed never to be read as a whole.

scenes.exactWording: Someone had hidden a book inside other books, like a secret distributed across a library's entire nervous system
scenes.reusableWording: Distributed text as intellectual puzzle and metaphor for hidden knowledge

title: 013. Signal and Noise
author: Priya Mehta
character_energy: Patient devotion to an uncertain purpose
character_note: Radio astronomer's professional patience mirrors personal inability to reach out
why_it_fits: Uses the physics of radio astronomy to explore human loneliness with scientific precision
summary: A radio astronomer at a remote Chilean observatory has spent eleven years monitoring a faint, possibly artificial signal from a star system forty light-years away. The signal has never repeated in a way that confirms intelligence, but it has never fully resolved into natural phenomena either. The story follows a single night shift where equipment upgrades might finally provide enough resolution to settle the question. Between calibration runs, the astronomer reflects on the relationships that have atrophied during her vigil — a marriage that couldn't survive the desert, friendships reduced to annual emails, a daughter who calls on birthdays. The signal's ambiguity becomes a mirror for the astronomer's own inability to send the messages that matter most.

scenes.exactWording: Forty light-years. The signal she was listening for had already been travelling longer than she'd been alive. Her text to her daughter had been travelling for three days.
scenes.reusableWording: Cosmic communication timescales contrasted with personal communication failures

title: 014. The Beekeeper's Audit
author: Margaret Finch
character_energy: Pragmatic stubbornness dressed in politeness
character_note: Beekeeper uses the inspector's own regulations against him with devastating precision
why_it_fits: Finds genuine comedy in the collision between agricultural tradition and modern regulation
summary: A county health inspector arrives to audit a seventy-year-old beekeeper's operation following a neighbour's complaint about 'excessive bee activity.' What follows is a comic masterclass in passive resistance, as the beekeeper — who has kept bees for fifty years and knows every regulation better than the inspector — guides the young official through a tour that technically complies with every request while making the inspection as uncomfortable as possible. Hives are opened at precisely the angle that encourages guard bees to investigate clipboards. Tea is offered at moments requiring two free hands. The story builds to a gentle resolution where the inspector, now stung three times and educated about pollination ecology, withdraws the complaint and asks if he can come back to learn about honey extraction.

scenes.exactWording: She opened the hive facing the wind, which was technically correct and practically catastrophic for anyone holding paperwork
scenes.reusableWording: Technical compliance weaponised as passive resistance

title: 015. Tidal Patterns
author: Aoife Brennan
character_energy: Geological patience applied to personal loss
character_note: Geologist processes family history through the lens of coastal erosion
why_it_fits: Parallels between geological and personal erosion create a quietly devastating dual narrative
summary: A geologist returns to the west coast of Ireland to measure the erosion rate of the cliffs near her childhood home — the same cliffs that have now consumed the garden where her recently deceased father taught her to identify rock formations. The story alternates between precise scientific measurements of retreat rates and childhood memories anchored to landmarks that no longer exist. A swimming spot has become open ocean. The path to the beach now ends in a vertical drop. Her father's shed, once thirty metres from the edge, fell into the sea two winters ago. The work of quantifying the loss in centimetres per year becomes a way of processing a grief that resists direct expression.

scenes.exactWording: The cliff had retreated fourteen centimetres since her last visit. Her father had retreated all at once.
scenes.reusableWording: Geological measurement as emotional displacement activity

title: 016. The Repair Shop
author: Kenji Morales
character_energy: Stubborn craftsmanship as quiet activism
character_note: Repair technician sees each fixed device as a small act of resistance against planned obsolescence
why_it_fits: Makes the mundane act of electronics repair into a compelling narrative about sustainability and skill
summary: In a near-future where manufacturer-locked devices are legally unrepairable, an underground repair shop operates from the back of a laundromat in East London. The story follows three devices brought in on a single Saturday: a child's tablet containing the only video recordings of a grandmother who has passed, a hearing aid whose manufacturer went bankrupt, and a vintage synthesiser that a musician needs for a recording session that evening. Each repair becomes a story about the relationship between people and their objects, the knowledge embedded in physical things, and the quiet violence of designed obsolescence. The technician's skill is portrayed with the same reverence usually reserved for fine art — soldering as a form of care.

scenes.exactWording: The solder flowed like it remembered where it was supposed to go. Thirty years of practice in his hands.
scenes.reusableWording: Craft mastery as embodied knowledge and act of preservation

title: 017. Counterpoint
author: Sofia Lindqvist
character_energy: Competitive fire tempered by artistic honesty
character_note: Rivals who understand each other's playing better than their own
why_it_fits: Captures the specific tension between competition and artistic communion in classical music
summary: Two pianists who have been rivals since conservatory meet again at a prestigious international competition. The story alternates between their perspectives as they prepare for the final round, each haunted by the other's interpretation of the same Rachmaninoff concerto. Through parallel practice sessions, the narrative reveals how their rivalry has shaped their artistry — each has spent years developing techniques specifically designed to surpass the other, inadvertently pushing both to heights neither would have reached alone. The competition itself is rendered not as a battle but as a conversation, each performance responding to what the other played. The judges' decision matters less than the moment backstage when one says to the other: 'I could never have played it that way without knowing you would be listening.'

scenes.exactWording: She played the adagio knowing he would hear every choice she made. It was the most honest she had ever been.
scenes.reusableWording: Artistic rivalry as the deepest form of mutual understanding

title: 018. The Last Cartographer of Mars
author: Liu Xiuying
character_energy: Dignified obsolescence in the face of automation
character_note: Human cartographer's subjective maps contain truths the AI surveys miss
why_it_fits: Uses planetary cartography to explore what is lost when human subjectivity is removed from observation
summary: On a Mars colony where satellite AI produces perfect topographic maps in real time, one human cartographer continues to walk the terrain and draw by hand. The colony administration considers her role redundant and schedules it for elimination. In her final season, she maps a region the satellites have catalogued as 'featureless basalt plain' and produces a document that includes wind patterns visible only at ground level, soil colour variations that indicate subsurface water, and the paths worn by colonists taking shortcuts between habitation modules — human desire lines on an alien world. The story argues, through her meticulous work, that a map is not just a representation of terrain but a record of attention, and that what a human notices is fundamentally different from what a sensor detects.

scenes.exactWording: The satellite saw the rock. She saw the shadow the rock cast at 4pm, when the children used it as a landmark on their way home.
scenes.reusableWording: Human observation capturing contextual meaning invisible to automated systems

title: 019. Grandmother's Algorithm
author: Nkechi Obi
character_energy: Ancestral wisdom smuggled into modern systems
character_note: Data scientist recognises grandmother's farming rules as a sophisticated algorithm
why_it_fits: Reframes indigenous knowledge systems as computational thinking with devastating effectiveness
summary: A Nigerian data scientist working for an agricultural AI company realises that her grandmother's traditional planting rules — which she has always dismissed as superstition — encode a sophisticated crop rotation and companion planting algorithm that outperforms the company's machine learning model. The story follows her attempt to integrate this knowledge into the commercial system without having it stripped of its cultural context and patented by the corporation. Flashbacks to childhood afternoons in her grandmother's garden interweave with present-day board meetings where she must translate oral tradition into the language of intellectual property law. The central tension is not whether the knowledge works, but who gets to own it and in what form it survives.

scenes.exactWording: Her grandmother had never used the word 'algorithm.' She called it 'what the soil remembers.'
scenes.reusableWording: Traditional ecological knowledge reframed as computational system

title: 020. Frequency Response
author: Oscar Petersen
character_energy: Creative reinvention forced by physical limitation
character_note: Sound engineer's hearing loss transforms his understanding of music from auditory to tactile
why_it_fits: Explores how disability can reshape artistic practice in unexpected and generative ways
summary: A recording engineer experiencing progressive hearing loss in the high frequencies develops a new approach to sound mixing that relies on vibration, visual waveform analysis, and the physical sensation of bass through studio monitors. Rather than retiring, he becomes sought after for a mixing style that other engineers cannot replicate — his mixes have an unusual warmth and physical presence because he is quite literally feeling the music rather than hearing it in the conventional sense. The story follows a session with a sceptical young band who initially resist his methods but gradually recognise that his 'limitation' produces something their fully hearing engineers never achieved: music that moves through the body before it reaches the ear.

scenes.exactWording: He pressed his palms flat on the speaker cabinet and closed his eyes. 'There,' he said. 'The kick drum is fighting the bass. I can feel them arguing.'
scenes.reusableWording: Tactile music perception revealing sonic relationships inaudible to conventional listening

title: 021. The Crossing Guard's Map
author: Patricia Huang
character_energy: Invisible expertise from years of standing still
character_note: Crossing guard knows the neighbourhood's emotional weather better than anyone
why_it_fits: Finds depth in the most overlooked urban role, demonstrating how stillness enables deep observation
summary: A crossing guard who has worked the same intersection for twenty-two years narrates a single morning shift, revealing the extraordinary depth of knowledge accumulated through decades of standing in one place. She knows which marriages are in trouble by how couples time their departures. She can predict rain by which dog walkers take the short route. She has watched children grow from stroller passengers to teenagers who pretend not to see her. The story is a love letter to the kind of knowledge that comes only from sustained, patient attention to a single place — a form of expertise that no algorithm or satellite can replicate.

scenes.exactWording: The Hendersons left the house two minutes apart now. Last year it was together.
scenes.reusableWording: Micro-observations from a fixed vantage point revealing hidden neighbourhood dynamics

title: 022. Deep Roots
author: Birgitta Holm
character_energy: Scientific wonder battling institutional cynicism
character_note: Mycologist's discovery challenges both her career and her understanding of intelligence
why_it_fits: Translates mycological research into narrative without sacrificing scientific accuracy or emotional depth
summary: A mycologist studying fungal networks in a Swedish old-growth forest discovers that the mycorrhizal web connecting the trees is not just transferring nutrients but encoding information in chemical patterns that resemble a primitive form of communication. Her university dismisses the finding as anthropomorphism, but a timber company takes notice — if the network is 'intelligent,' logging the forest could trigger regulatory protections they want to pre-empt. The scientist finds herself in a race to publish before the company clear-cuts the study site. The story weaves between the quiet, patient work of underground sampling and the noisy politics of academic publishing and corporate lobbying, arguing that the slowest forms of life may have the most to teach us about cooperation.

scenes.exactWording: The network had been running for four hundred years on a protocol that required no updates, no patches, no restarts. She wondered what her university's IT department would say.
scenes.reusableWording: Ancient biological systems compared to fragile modern technological ones

title: 023. Paper Hands
author: Fumiko Tanaka
character_energy: Coded defiance through traditional craft
character_note: Paper folder embeds messages in origami patterns shared through seemingly innocent gifts
why_it_fits: Transforms a traditional craft into a vehicle for resistance narrative with historical plausibility
summary: In wartime Osaka, a paper artisan develops a system of coded messages hidden in the fold patterns of origami figures distributed to neighbours as gifts. Each crane, frog, or flower contains information about food caches, safe houses, and the movements of military police, readable only to those who know which folds to examine. The story follows a single week as a crackdown intensifies and the artisan must decide whether to destroy her folding patterns — her life's artistic work — to protect the network. The narrative pays meticulous attention to the physical act of folding: the grain of the paper, the precision required for each crease, the way a single misalignment can collapse an entire structure. The paper becomes a metaphor for the fragile systems that hold communities together under pressure.

scenes.exactWording: A crane with seventeen folds meant safety. Eighteen meant run.
scenes.reusableWording: Craft technique repurposed as coded communication under surveillance

title: 024. The Night Market Economist
author: Thanh Nguyen
character_energy: Street-level economic genius operating outside formal systems
character_note: Noodle vendor's pricing strategy reveals sophisticated understanding of local microeconomics
why_it_fits: Makes informal economic systems visible and legible through character-driven narrative
summary: A university economics professor visits a Saigon night market to gather data for a paper on informal economies and discovers that the noodle vendor she interviews has developed a pricing model more sophisticated than anything in her textbooks. The vendor adjusts prices based on weather, the emotional state of the queue, which other stalls are open, and a complex system of credit extended to regular customers that functions as an unofficial community banking system. The professor's attempts to formalise this knowledge into academic language consistently fail to capture its elegance. The story is structured as the professor's field notes, increasingly annotated with the vendor's corrections and additions, until the two voices merge into a hybrid document that is neither academic paper nor street wisdom but something more useful than either.

scenes.exactWording: She charged less when it rained. Not because fewer people came, but because the ones who came needed the warmth more.
scenes.reusableWording: Informal pricing incorporating emotional and social variables invisible to formal economics

title: 025. Last Light on the Loom
author: Isla MacLeod
character_energy: Hands remembering what the mind is forgetting
character_note: Weaver with early dementia retains craft knowledge long after other memories fade
why_it_fits: Explores embodied knowledge and memory through the specific, tactile medium of handweaving
summary: A Harris Tweed weaver in her eighties, experiencing the early stages of dementia, can no longer remember her daughter's name on difficult days but can still produce flawless cloth on a loom she has operated for sixty years. Her granddaughter, a textile student, returns to the Outer Hebrides to document the weaving patterns before they are lost — only to discover that the knowledge cannot be captured in notation or video. It lives in the rhythm of the pedals, the tension of the warp under specific fingers, the sound the shuttle makes when the weave is right. The story is about the difference between information and knowledge, and the particular cruelty of a disease that erases one while leaving the other untouched. The final scene — grandmother and granddaughter weaving side by side in silence — is a transmission that requires no words.

scenes.exactWording: Her hands knew the pattern. Her hands had always known the pattern. It was everything else that was leaving.
scenes.reusableWording: Embodied craft knowledge persisting after declarative memory fails
